lessons.title: print: showing on screen
lessons.body: `print()` is the command that shows something on the screen — it's how the program 'talks' to you. You put inside the parentheses what you want to display: text (always in quotes), a number, a variable, or several items separated by commas. It's every programmer's first tool: when something doesn't work, dropping a `print()` in the middle of your code to see what's happening is the simplest way to investigate.
lessons.example:
# Show text (goes in quotes)
print('Hello, world!')

# Show a number (numbers don't use quotes)
print(42)

# Several items at once, separated by commas:
# print puts a space between them automatically
print('The answer is', 42)

# An empty print skips a blank line
print()
print('The end.')

lessons.title: Variables: creating and changing
lessons.body: A variable stores a value for you to use later. It's created the moment you assign a value with the `=` sign — which here does NOT mean 'equals', but 'receive this value'. Read `age = 21` as 'age receives 21'. You can change the value at any time: the new assignment replaces the old one. And you can use a variable's current value to compute the next one — even its own, as in `points = points + 10`. Naming rules: use letters, numbers and `_`, start with a letter, no spaces or accents, and pick names that explain what the variable holds (`total_price` beats `x`).
lessons.example:
# '=' means 'receive', not 'is equal to'
name = 'Ann'      # the variable name receives the text 'Ann'
age = 21          # the variable age receives the number 21
print(name, age)

# Reassigning replaces the previous value
age = 22
print(age)        # 22 (the 21 is gone)

# Use the current value to compute the new one
points = 100
points = points + 50   # take 100, add 50, store 150 back
print(points)          # 150

# f-strings insert variables inside text (note the f before the quotes)
print(f'{name} is {age} years old with {points} points')

lessons.title: Data types
lessons.body: Every value in Python has a type, and the type defines what you can do with it. The four basic types are: `int` (whole number, like 7), `float` (number with decimals, like 3.14), `str` (text, always in quotes) and `bool` (logical, only `True` or `False`). You don't declare the type — Python figures it out from the value you write. The `type()` function shows the type of any value. Knowing the type is essential: `'10' + '5'` joins two texts and gives `'105'`, while `10 + 5` adds two numbers and gives `15`.
lessons.example:
# int: whole number
amount = 7
print(type(amount))        # <class 'int'>

# float: number with decimals
price = 3.14
print(type(price))         # <class 'float'>

# str: text, always in quotes
name = 'Ann'
print(type(name))          # <class 'str'>

# bool: only True or False (capitalized)
active = True
print(type(active))        # <class 'bool'>

# The type changes how '+' behaves:
print('10' + '5')          # '105'  (joined texts)
print(10 + 5)              # 15      (added numbers)

lessons.title: Converting types
lessons.body: Often a value comes in the wrong type and you need to convert it. The most common case: numbers typed by the user arrive as text, and you can't do math with them until they become numbers. The conversion functions are named after the target type: `int()` turns something into a whole number, `float()` into a decimal, and `str()` into text. If you try to add text to a number, Python raises an error — conversion fixes that. Careful: only convert to a number something that really is a number; `int('abc')` raises an error.
lessons.example:
# Text that represents a number does NO math while it's text
age_text = '21'
# age_text + 1  ->  error! can't add text to a number

# Converting text to a whole number
age = int(age_text)
print(age + 1)             # 22

# Converting text to a decimal number
price = float('19.90')
print(price * 2)           # 39.8

# Converting a number to text (handy for joining with other text)
score = 3
print('Goals: ' + str(score))   # 'Goals: 3'

# int() of a float drops the decimals (it does not round)
print(int(3.99))           # 3

lessons.title: Numbers and operations
lessons.body: Python has integers (`int`) and decimals (`float`). The operators are `+` (add), `-` (subtract), `*` (multiply), `/` (division, which always results in a float), `//` (integer division, which drops the remainder), `%` (remainder of the division) and `**` (power). Math follows the usual order — multiplication and division before addition and subtraction — and you can use parentheses to force any order you want. The `//` and `%` operators look odd at first, but they're very useful: `%` tells you whether a number is even (`n % 2 == 0`) and `//` splits amounts into groups.
lessons.example:
# Basic operations
a = 7
b = 2

print(a + b)   # 9
print(a / b)   # 3.5  (division always returns a float)
print(a // b)  # 3    (integer division drops the remainder)
print(a % b)   # 1    (remainder of the division)
print(a ** b)  # 49   (7 to the power of 2)

# Parentheses change the order of operations
print(2 + 3 * 4)     # 14  (multiplies first)
print((2 + 3) * 4)   # 20  (parentheses first)

# Common trick: an even number leaves remainder 0 when divided by 2
print(10 % 2 == 0)   # True  (10 is even)
print(7 % 2 == 0)    # False (7 is odd)

lessons.title: Reading input with input()
lessons.body: `input()` makes the program stop and wait for the user to type something on the keyboard. The text you pass inside the parentheses appears as the prompt. One crucial detail: `input()` ALWAYS returns text (`str`), even if the person types numbers. So, to do math with what was typed, you need to convert it with `int()` or `float()`. Forgetting this conversion is one of the most common beginner mistakes. (In this app the input is simulated, but the concept is the same as in any Python program.)
lessons.example:
# input() shows the prompt and returns what was typed (as text)
name = input('What is your name? ')
print(f'Hello, {name}!')

# Since input() returns TEXT, convert before calculating
age_text = input('Your age: ')
age = int(age_text)               # text -> whole number
print(f'Next year you will be {age + 1}')

# You can convert on the same line:
price = float(input('Product price: '))
print(f'With 10% off: {price * 0.9}')

lessons.title: Strings: working with text
lessons.body: Strings are text between quotes (single or double, it doesn't matter). You can join them with `+` (concatenate), repeat them with `*`, and measure their length with `len()`. Each character has a position, starting at zero: in `'python'`, the `'p'` is at position 0. You access a character with `text[position]` and take pieces (slices) with `text[start:end]` — the end is not included. Strings also have methods, which are functions built into them: `.upper()`, `.lower()`, `.strip()` (removes spaces from the ends), `.replace()` (swaps parts). Important: methods don't change the original string, they return a new one.
lessons.example:
phrase = 'python is fun'

# len() counts the characters (including spaces)
print(len(phrase))         # 13

# Positions start at 0
print(phrase[0])           # 'p'  (first character)
print(phrase[-1])          # 'n'  (last character)

# Slices take pieces: [start:end] (end is not included)
print(phrase[0:6])         # 'python'

# Methods transform the string (without changing the original)
print(phrase.upper())      # 'PYTHON IS FUN'
print(phrase.replace('fun', 'awesome'))
print(phrase)              # still 'python is fun'

# Join and repeat
print('ab' + 'cd')         # 'abcd'
print('=' * 10)            # '=========='

lessons.title: Booleans and comparisons
lessons.body: The `bool` type has only two values: `True` and `False`. Most of the time they come from comparisons. The comparison operators are: `==` (equal — careful, that's TWO equal signs; a single one is assignment!), `!=` (not equal), `>`, `<`, `>=` and `<=`. You combine conditions with `and` (true only if both are), `or` (true if at least one is) and `not` (inverts). Booleans are the foundation of the decisions (`if`) you'll see in the next chapter.
lessons.example:
age = 20
has_ticket = True

# Comparisons result in True or False
print(age == 20)       # True  (== compares; = would assign!)
print(age != 18)       # True  (it's different from 18)
print(age >= 21)       # False

# and: needs both conditions true
print(age >= 18 and has_ticket)   # True

# or: just one needs to be true
print(age < 12 or has_ticket)     # True

# not: inverts the value
print(not has_ticket)             # False

lessons.title: Comments and good names
lessons.body: Comments are pieces that Python ignores — they exist for YOU (and whoever reads the code later) to understand what's going on. Everything after a `#` on a line is a comment. Use them to explain the 'why' of something, not the obvious. Just as important is choosing good variable names: `total_price` says what it holds; `x` or `a1` say nothing. Well-commented code with good names is code you'll still understand three months from now. Programming is communication — with the computer and with people.
lessons.example:
# This is a comment: Python ignores this entire line

price = 100
discount = 0.15   # 15% birthday discount

# Good names make the code almost self-explanatory:
final_price = price * (1 - discount)
print(final_price)   # 85.0

# Compare with the bad-names version (it works, but nobody gets it):
x = 100
y = 0.15
z = x * (1 - y)
print(z)             # 85.0  -> but what is z?

# Always prefer names that explain the content.

exercises.title: Your first greeting
exercises.prompt: Create a variable called `message` with the text 'Hello, Python!' and print it on the screen with print().
exercises.starterCode: # Create the variable and use print()

tests.name: variable message exists with the right text
tests.code: assert message == 'Hello, Python!', f"expected 'Hello, Python!', got {message!r}"

hints: Text in Python goes between single or double quotes.

exercises.title: Sum function
exercises.prompt: Write a function `add(a, b)` that returns the sum of the two numbers.
hints: Use the return keyword to give back the result.

exercises.title: Double the number
exercises.prompt: Write a function `double(n)` that returns twice n.
hints: Multiply n by 2 and return the result.

exercises.title: Shouting the text
exercises.prompt: Write a function `shout(text)` that returns the text in UPPERCASE followed by '!'. E.g.: shout('hi') → 'HI!'
hints: The .upper() method makes the string uppercase.
Join strings with +.

exercises.title: Rectangle area
exercises.prompt: Write a function `area(base, height)` that returns the area of a rectangle.
hints: The area is base times height (base * height).

exercises.title: Average of two numbers
exercises.prompt: Write a function `average(a, b)` that returns the arithmetic mean of two numbers.
hints: Add the two numbers and divide by 2.

exercises.title: Seconds to minutes
exercises.prompt: Write a function `minutes(seconds)` that returns how many whole minutes fit in the given number of seconds. E.g.: minutes(130) → 2
hints: Integer division // drops the remainder: 130 // 60 == 2.

exercises.title: Name initial
exercises.prompt: Write a function `initial(name)` that returns the first letter of the name in uppercase. E.g.: initial('ana') → 'A'
hints: name[0] takes the first letter.
Use .upper() to make it uppercase.

exercises.title: Discounted price
exercises.prompt: Write a function `discounted(price, percent)` that returns the price after applying the percentage discount. E.g.: discounted(100, 20) → 80.0
hints: Discount amount = price * percent / 100.
Subtract the discount from the price.

exercises.title: Text length
exercises.prompt: Write a function `length(text)` that returns how many characters the text has.
hints: The len() function counts the characters of a string.

exercises.title: Full name
exercises.prompt: Write a function `full_name(first, last)` that returns both names joined, separated by a space. E.g.: full_name('Ana', 'Silva') → 'Ana Silva'
hints: Use an f-string: f'{first} {last}'.
Or concatenate with +: first + ' ' + last.

exercises.title: Square of a number
exercises.prompt: Write a function `square(n)` that returns n squared.
hints: The ** operator raises to a power: n ** 2.

exercises.title: Change
exercises.prompt: Write a function `change(paid, price)` that returns how much change should be given back.
hints: The change is the amount paid minus the price (paid - price).

exercises.title: Average of three numbers
exercises.prompt: Write a function `average3(a, b, c)` that returns the arithmetic mean of the three numbers.
hints: Add the three numbers and divide by 3.

exercises.title: Reversed text
exercises.prompt: Write a function `reverse(text)` that returns the text backwards. E.g.: reverse('python') → 'nohtyp'
hints: Slicing with a negative step reverses the string: text[::-1].

exercises.title: Repeat text
exercises.prompt: Write a function `repeat(text, times)` that returns the text repeated the given number of times. E.g.: repeat('ab', 3) → 'ababab'
hints: The * operator repeats strings: text * times.

exercises.title: Celsius to Fahrenheit
exercises.prompt: Write a function `celsius_to_fahrenheit(c)` that converts a temperature in Celsius to Fahrenheit. Formula: F = C * 9/5 + 32
hints: Apply the formula directly: c * 9 / 5 + 32.

exercises.title: Spaces to hyphens
exercises.prompt: Write a function `text_with_hyphens(text)` that returns the text with every space replaced by a hyphen. E.g.: text_with_hyphens('a b c') → 'a-b-c'

tests.name: text_with_hyphens('a b c') == 'a-b-c'
tests.code: assert text_with_hyphens('a b c') == 'a-b-c', f"got {text_with_hyphens('a b c')!r}"

hints: The .replace(' ', '-') method swaps each space for a hyphen.
